coredata.dc:title: Advances in the understanding of the electron-donating properties of carbon-based nanomaterials electrogenerated from graphite
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Veloso, A.D.
Ferraria, A.M.
Videira, R.A.
Oliveira, M.C.
coredata.dc:description: In a seminal paper, we demonstrated voltammetrically that carbon nanomaterials electrogenerated from graphite in specific carboxylic acid buffers show electron-donating properties, exhibiting a semiquinone-like state. Unexpectedly, after the terminus of the electrogeneration process, its concentration was found to increase when citrate was used as the synthesis buffer electrolyte. In this paper, we revisit and provide a mechanistic interpretation of this phenomenon. Beyond the formation of semiquinone-like species during the galvanostatic synthesis, we present voltammetric and spectroscopic (XPS) evidence for the formation of a non-electroactive superoxide-quinone adduct. It was found that the two entities, semiquinone and superoxide-quinone adduct, can convert into each other in a redox cycle mediated by molecular oxygen, which allowed rationalising the temporal evolution of the voltammetric response. It was concluded that the carboxylic acid buffer used in the electrochemical synthesis determines the quinonoid form of the carbon nanomaterial. In tartaric buffer solution, the semiquinone form predominates, whereas, in citrate and malic buffers, the quinone-superoxide adduct and an oxidised quinoid form are the dominant species, respectively. Upon the electrolyte removal, the electro-donating capacity of the nanomaterials is lost, and the nanomaterial is converted into a form with electron-accepting activity. Overall, this study highlights the crucial role of the electrolyte in shaping and sustaining the electro-donating properties of hydrophilic carbon nanomaterials in aqueous media.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Portugal institutions: Core Hybrid journals RAP 2025
coredata.dcterms:subject: Superoxide
Electroactivity
Redox-active
Carbon dots
Radical stability
XPS

Andreia D. Veloso thanks FCT, MCTES, ESF, and EU through the individual research Ph.D. for the Ph.D. scholarship (SFRH/BD/138425/2018). Romeu A. Videira thanks FCT for funding through CEECINST/00136/2021/CP2820/CT0001 DOI 10.54499/CEECINST/00136/2021/CP2820/CT0001

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cyclic voltammograms (first scan) of EHC@tartaric (pH 3.7), EHC@citric (pH 4.7), and EHC@malic (pH 4.2) solutions, recorded immediately after synthesis. υ = 50 mV/s.

Time evolution of the cyclic voltammograms recorded in EHC@citric, EHC@malic, and EHC@tartaric solutions on a glassy carbon electrode at a scan rate of 50 mV/s (a-c). Plot of the ratio jp/jp0 vs post-synthesis time, where jp represents the current oxidation peak at the time t and jp0 corresponds to the oxidation peak current recorded immediately after synthesis (a´-c´).

Monitoring of the O2 reduction current at -0.60 V, using a Clark-type electrode, in different EHC solutions subjected to a gradual increase in temperature: a) EHC@citric b) EHC@malic; c) EHC@tartaric. For each EHC solution, a control was performed by replacing the nanomaterial solution with the respective buffer solution used as the electrolyte (pH adjusted to match that of the nanomaterial solutions).

Cyclic voltammograms on a glassy carbon electrode of EHC@citric (a), EHC@malic (b), and EHC@tartaric (c) solutions recorded immediately after synthesis, immediately after O2 saturation, and after O2 removal, respectively. υ = 5 mV/s.

Cyclic voltammograms on a glassy carbon electrode of EHC@citric, b) EHC@malic, and c) EHC@tartaric solutions after synthesis, and at different times after O2 saturation. υ = 50 mV/s. The shaded blue area corresponds to the oxygen reduction region (denoted as jORR).

Cyclic voltammograms in 0.10 M phosphate buffer (pH 7) solution of glassy carbon electrodes modified with dialysed EHC@citric (a), EHC@malic (b), and EHC@tartaric solutions (c). Includes voltammograms of unmodified electrodes (dashed line). υ = 50 mV/s.
XPS C 1s region in EHC@malic (a). Atomic concentrations (%) and atomic ratios in EHC@citric, EHC@tartaric, and EHC@malic samples (b).
Schematic representation of the oxygen-mediated redox cycle involving the EHC species.

head.ce:title: Advances in the understanding of the electron-donating properties of carbon-based nanomaterials electrogenerated from graphite

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In a seminal paper, we demonstrated voltammetrically that carbon nanomaterials electrogenerated from graphite in specific carboxylic acid buffers show electron-donating properties, exhibiting a semiquinone-like state. Unexpectedly, after the terminus of the electrogeneration process, its concentration was found to increase when citrate was used as the synthesis buffer electrolyte. In this paper, we revisit and provide a mechanistic interpretation of this phenomenon.

Beyond the formation of semiquinone-like species during the galvanostatic synthesis, we present voltammetric and spectroscopic (XPS) evidence for the formation of a non-electroactive superoxide-quinone adduct. It was found that the two entities, semiquinone and superoxide-quinone adduct, can convert into each other in a redox cycle mediated by molecular oxygen, which allowed rationalising the temporal evolution of the voltammetric response.

It was concluded that the carboxylic acid buffer used in the electrochemical synthesis determines the quinonoid form of the carbon nanomaterial. In tartaric buffer solution, the semiquinone form predominates, whereas, in citrate and malic buffers, the quinone-superoxide adduct and an oxidised quinoid form are the dominant species, respectively. Upon the electrolyte removal, the electro-donating capacity of the nanomaterials is lost, and the nanomaterial is converted into a form with electron-accepting activity.
Overall, this study highlights the crucial role of the electrolyte in shaping and sustaining the electro-donating properties of hydrophilic carbon nanomaterials in aqueous media.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon material is a ubiquitous material in the history of electrochemistry, extensively employed in the fabrication of classic electrodes (e.g. graphite, graphite-paste, graphite-ink, glassy carbon). Its widespread use is attributed to its excellent electrical conductivity, wide potential window, electrochemical inertness, and chemical stability [1]. Despite these qualities, nanostructured carbon materials offer opportunities that bulk carbon does not [2–5]. For example, the ability to modulate the heterogeneous electron transfer rate at the electrode/electrolyte interface based on the different contributions of edge-plane and basal-plane-like sites to the electron transfer reaction [6–8]. Typically, the electrochemistry of carbon-based nanomaterials is investigated using the nanomaterial as an electrode material. However, when it is dispersed in the solution side, particularly in aqueous medium, its behaviour remains largely unexplored. The primary reason for this is the low solubility of the carbon-based nanomaterial in water.

A recent study demonstrated that carbon-based nanomaterials that are generated electrochemically in the early stages of graphite electrode polarisation show, in solution, a unique voltammetric signature [9]. Depending on the nature of the electrolyte used in the electrochemical generation, the voltammograms bring to light the electron-donating or electron-accepting properties of the nanomaterial. In a phosphate buffer, it was evidenced its electron-accepting ability [10], whereas the electron-donating properties were exhibited if the synthesis was carried out in malic, citric, or tartaric buffers [9].

The electron-accepting ability was attributed to the presence of readily reducible quinones and epoxide functional groups, while the electron-donating properties were associated with semiquinone-like groups, despite their typical instability in aqueous media [11]. Another intriguing behavior of the electron-donating nanomaterials was observed after the termination of the nanomaterial generation process: a progressive increase in its concentration over the first 300 mi when the nanomaterial was generated in citrate buffer, contrasting with the decay observed when prepared in malic or tartaric buffers. In our earlier mechanistic interpretation, it was proposed that in the absence of an external driving force, hydrogen abstraction from citric acid by the co-generated hydrogen peroxide occurs, leading to the formation of an oxidized carboxyl radical [9]. This radical species was hypothesised to react with quinone-like EHC nanomaterial released from graphite, resulting in the increased concentration of the semiquinone-like EHC species.

To better understand the underlying mechanism, we revisit the electrochemical characterisation over time and complement it with the XPS analysis to provide a detailed insight into the nanomaterial composition. The referred nanomaterial, named Electrogenerated Hydrophilic Carbon (EHC), consists of a disordered carbon network with a core of aromatic sp² carbon atoms, enriched with oxygen-containing functional groups and sodium cations. Typically, these nanomaterials assemble into large aggregates, with the small units having dimensions comparable to carbon dots [12].

EHC nanomaterials were prepared from a graphite anode (99.997 %, Goodfellow) via an electrochemical method previously described in the literature [9]. Electrolysis was performed under galvanostatic polarisation for one hour (I= 60 mA) in a three-electrode cell, with both working and counter electrodes of graphite, and a saturated calomel electrode as the reference. The working electrode (∅= 10 mm) was immersed 2 cm into the solution, resulting in a current density of 8.5 mA cm-2. The anode and counter electrode were positioned in separate compartments, divided by a glass frit, with a spacing of approximately 6 cm between them. No agitation was applied during the electrode polarization process.

Three different carboxylic acid buffers were used as electrolytes: citric (0.089 M, pH 5.55), malic (0.20 M, pH 4.60), and tartaric (0.11 M, pH 4.37). To facilitate sample identification, the synthesised nanomaterials were designated by the acronym EHC@buffer, corresponding to the buffer solution used in their synthesis. The mass concentration of EHC nanomaterials was determined gravimetrically by the difference between the dry mass of the as-prepared nanomaterial solution and the dry mass of its electrolyte.

After the electrolysis was completed, the solution from the anodic compartment was collected and characterised by cyclic voltammetry using one-compartment electrochemical cell with a glassy carbon working electrode (Ø = 3 mm; ALS-Japan), a platinum counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl (3 M NaCl) reference electrode, to which the potentials are referenced. Unless otherwise stated, the solutions were deaerated for 30 min with argon before characterisation, and voltammograms were recorded under an argon stream.

To investigate the effect of post-synthesis storage time, the freshly prepared EHC solutions were divided into aliquots and stored in dark glass vials under an argon atmosphere at −18 °C. Before electrochemical characterisation, the vials were brought to room temperature and only then opened, after which 50 µL of the solution was immediately transferred onto a carbon screen-printed electrode (DRP-110, DropSens), and the voltammograms were recorded under air atmosphere.

To evaluate and compare the reactivity of the nanomaterials toward O₂, chronoamperometric assays were conducted using a Clark-type oxygen electrode in one-compartment cell. For this purpose, a platinum working electrode (ALS-Japan, Ø = 2 mm) was used onto which a drop of saturated potassium chloride solution was placed. Over this drop, a PTFE membrane (S4, Hansatech Instruments) previously hydrated in KCl (sat) was fixed to the electrode using a rubber O-ring. A platinum electrode was used as the secondary electrode, and an Ag/AgCl electrode (NaCl 3 M; ALS Co) served as the reference. To control the temperature of the electrolyte solution, the electrochemical cell was placed in a thermostatic water bath. The assays were performed under stirring (142 rpm) with an applied potential of -0.60 V. To determine the device's sensitivity under these conditions, chronoamperograms were recorded in a phosphate buffer solution (50 mM, pH 7) under normal atmospheric conditions, as well as in oxygen-saturated and deoxygenated solutions. Based on these results, the electrode sensitivity was calculated as 0.92 µA mol⁻¹ O₂ at 37 ⁰C.

To evaluate the effect of electrolyte removal on the electrochemical properties of the EHCs, the dialysed samples were also characterised using cyclic voltammetry. These assays were conducted using the one-compartment electrochemical cell previously described, but with a glassy carbon working electrode modified by the drop-casting methodology with the dialysed nanomaterials. For this, 10 µL of the aqueous EHC solution was placed onto the surface electrode and allowed to evaporate under an argon flow. A 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution at pH 7 was used as the electrolyte. Before the voltammetric assays, the solution was deoxygenated with argon for 30 min and kept under an argon flow throughout the experiment. Dialysis was carried out with a cellulose membrane (10 mm diameter, molecular weight cutoff of 3.5–5 kD, Biotech RC Tubing) against 500 mL of ultrapure water for approximately 48 h, under magnetic stirring, at room temperature, and in the dark.

The X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) characterisation was performed with a dual-anode XSAM800 spectrometer from Kratos. The samples were prepared by successively depositing drops of the sample solution onto a boron-doped Si (111) substrate (without pre-treatment) until it was uniformly covered with a thick, visible layer. The samples were dried under vacuum (0.1 bar) at room temperature and immediately transferred to the XPS chamber.

Spectra acquisition was performed with the Vision 2 software for Windows, version 2.2.9 (KRATOS), and peak analysis was conducted using the Solver program in Microsoft Excel. For the C 1s region, all the samples were fitted simultaneously, keeping the binding energy of the peaks constant while adjusting their area, full width at half maximum (FWHM), and Gaussian-Lorentzian percentage. Details of the operational conditions have been published elsewhere [13].

Voltammograms of EHC solutions generated in citric, malic, and tartaric buffers are shown in Fig. 1 . It is shown that the nanomaterial solution with the highest mass concentration, EHC@malic, presents the lowest peak current density, contrasting with EHC@tartaric, which has the lowest mass concentration but the highest peak current density. Since the current density reflects the quantity of the electroactive species, it can be concluded that only a fraction of the as-prepared electrogenerated nanomaterial is electroactive and that the highest fraction of electroactive nanomaterial is generated in tartaric buffer. However, as previously noted, the peak current density in EHC@tartaric undergoes a rapid decline over the subsequent 300 min, even when the solution is maintained under an argon atmosphere at room temperature [9]. This behaviour contrasts with the trend observed in EHC@citric, which shows an increase over the same period.

To assess whether these behaviours remains consistent over an extended period of 12 months, the stock solution of the synthesized nanomaterial—prepared in citric, malic, or tartaric buffer—was divided into aliquots. These aliquots were stored individually and later analysed independently by cyclic voltammetry.

Fig. 2 illustrates the obtained voltammograms, as well as the evolution of jp/jp0 over time. The results indicate that the trend initially observed during the first 300 min persists over a much longer period, but additional information is also revealed. Accordingly, the results show that the concentration of the electroactive species EHC@citric increases sharply over time, tripling the initial value after approximately three months. Following this period, the concentration decreases, reaching approximately 1.8 times the initial value by the end of the first year. During this decline, there are intervals where the current appears to stabilize, such as between days 120 and 180. For EHC@malic, there is also an increase in the oxidation current, but only detectable between days 15 and 90. After approximately 90 days, the oxidation peak intensity begins to decrease regularly again till a stabilization from day 200 is observed. For EHC@tartaric, there is an initial steep decrease of the electroactive species up to day 15. From day 15 to 30, the electroactive concentration of EHC@tartaric remains nearly unchanged, followed by a progressive decrease between days 30 and 200. After this, it stabilizes.

The final balance indicates that by the end of the first year, the concentration of electroactive species increased by 80 % in EHC@citric, decreased by 80 % in EHC@malic, and decreased by nearly 100 % in EHC@tartaric. Notable oscillations in the onset potential of EHC@tartaric samples (around 200 mV), while less intense in EHC@citric, also indicate some variability in the electroactivity of these nanomaterials.

To interpret these results, we hypothesised that at least two EHC species, interconvertible at open circuit potential, are present at the end of the nanomaterial production process: an electroactive EHC and a non-electroactive EHC, whose relative amounts depend on the electrolyte where the nanomaterial was generated.

To assess whether this interconversion is mediated by the O2 co-generated at the anode and dissolved in the solution, three experiments were conducted: a) monitoring dissolved O2 while gradually increasing the solution's temperature to accelerate any reaction that may occur between the gas and the EHC nanomaterial; b) tracking the voltammetric response over time after oxygen saturation of the solution; and c) comparing the effects of saturating and removing oxygen on the voltammetric response of EHC materials.

Monitoring of dissolved oxygen was conducted with a Clark-type electrode while a potential of -0.60 V was applied to promote the reduction of O₂, Fig. 3 . In these essays, it was used the as-prepared EHC solution (not deaerated). The recording began at room temperature (T = 23 ⁰C). After 500 s, the temperature was gradually increased at a rate of 1.4 °C/min until a final temperature of 46 °C was reached. For comparison, the same experiment was conducted with the electrolyte (control) used on the EHC preparation, but with pH adjusted to match that of the sample. The results reveal that, at the beginning of the recording, the reduction current intensity is higher in the control than in the EHC samples, suggesting a lower O2 concentration in the EHC solution. As the temperature increases, a divergence in the current profiles of the EHC samples and control is observed. In general, the temperature increase leads to a gradual rise in the oxygen reduction current in the control solutions, whereas in the sample solutions, a gradual decrease in the reduction current is observed, reaching nearly zero in EHC@tartaric.

This behavior indicates that, despite the enhanced mobility of oxygen molecules with rising temperatures, the presence of EHC nanomaterial significantly reduces the flux of oxygen molecules reaching the electrode. This observation aligns with the EHC nanomaterial's ability to adsorb or/and react with dissolved oxygen, even at room temperature. The rate of O2 depletion is more pronounced in the EHC@tartaric solution, as evidenced by the greater slope dI/dt. Although these tests demonstrate the nanomaterials' ability to interact with dissolved O₂, they do not allow determining whether the reactive species are in the electroactive or non-electroactive form of EHC.

To address this issue, the voltammetric response of the EHC samples was analysed at distinct stages: initially after synthesis, then following oxygen saturation, and finally after oxygen removal by argon purging, Fig. 4 . A scan rate of 5 mV s-1 was used to approach steady-state conditions. The marked decrease in the principal oxidation peak observed upon oxygen saturation strongly suggests that O2 reacts with electroactive EHC nanomaterial, regardless of whether it is formed in citric, tartaric, or malic buffers. This behaviour reinforces the hypothesis that the electroactive EHC nanomaterial is a semiquinone-type species, as previously suggested [9]. Notably, although an additional electroactive group is detected in EHC@citric at 0.44 V (peak Ia), this new group does not react with O2. This secondary peak is likely assigned to the oxidation of hydroxyl groups attached to the aromatic rings [14].

Remarkably, after deaerating EHC@tartaric and EHC@citric solutions, the main oxidation peak reappears in the voltammograms, although at a lower intensity. This behaviour suggests that the electroactive form of EHC nanomaterial, along with its non-electroactive oxidation product, can undergo an oxygen-mediated redox cycle. Despite the reversibility of such reaction, it is not possible to fully recover the initial voltammetric response after O₂ removal from the solution. This situation is especially evident in the EHC@malic sample. After oxidation, the non-electroactive form of EHC likely evolves into a highly stable form, preventing the reverse reaction from occurring, Scheme 1 . It is important to emphasise that, although hydrogen peroxide is known to be formed via this parallel pathway [9], the hypothesis that hydrogen peroxide reacts with the carboxylic buffer and gives rise to a reductant agent that would reduce EHCoxid regenerating the electroactive species is not supported by assays where EHCox was prepared and hydrogen peroxide was subsequently added in the presence of the carboxylic acid buffer (Figs. S1-S2).

To track the time-dependent interconversion between the non-electroactive and electroactive forms, the voltammetric response in the oxygen-saturated solution was monitored over time, as shown in Fig. 5 . The potential region of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is highlighted to simultaneously monitor the variation in oxygen content (blue-shaded area). The voltammograms at 50 mV s-1 reveal that as soon as the solution is saturated with oxygen, the peak intensity immediately decreases, confirming the reactivity observed at 5 mV s-1. Note that during these assays the solution was maintained under an O2 atmosphere, and the working electrode was cleaned before each measurement. It is shown that the voltammograms of EHC@citric and EHC@malic solutions show a gradual decrease in the oxidation peak intensity over time, Fig. 5a and 5b, respectively. This decrease is followed by the decrease of the ORR current, further supporting the hypothesis that the species that is responsible for the oxidation peak also reacts with O2. As this reaction progresses, the EHC becomes less electroactive, as indicated by the shift of Ep to more positive values, particularly noticeable in EHC@citric. After 40 min in EHC@citric and 100 min in EHC@malic, respectively, this process is reversed, showing an increase in the oxidation peak current, a concomitant rise in the ORR current, and a shift in the peak potential to more negative values.

To interpret these results, a set of reactions are proposed: reaction (1), which is a reversible homogeneous reaction involving the electroactive nanomaterial (EHCred), and reaction (2), which is responsible for the formation of hydrogen peroxide.
(1)

Reaction (1), in the forward direction, decreases the concentration of [EHCred] •−, causing a shift of Ep to more positive potentials. It is proposed that the non-electroactive form of EHC, involved in the reaction (1), is an adduct formed by the combination of EHCox and the superoxide radical anion, [EHCox-O2]•−. It is not expected that the superoxide radical anion exists in its free form, as its presence was not detected upon the addition of nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) (Fig. 3S). In the presence of O2 •−, it would lead to the formation of a strong-blue insoluble product (formazan) [15]. Evidence for the formation of the superoxide adduct arises from other observations. In addition to the detection of hydrogen peroxide and the yellow colour of the solution (Fig. 4S), characteristic of superoxide-containing solutions [16], its formation is further corroborated by the strong gas release (O2) observed during vacuum evaporation of the samples. This effect is particularly noticeable in EHC@malic and EHC@citric, resulting in a foamy residue (Fig. 5S). Further evidence of this adduct is also given by the XPS data (vide infra). To the best of our knowledge, this type of adduct has never been suggested nor studied before in carbon-based nanomaterials. Nevertheless, a related mechanism has been proposed in the literature for the reaction of O2 with 1,4-semiquinone radicals, involving the reversible formation of an O2​ adduct at the carbon bearing the OH group. This process yields a peroxyl radical stabilized by intramolecular hydrogen bonding [17].

Regarding the evolution of the voltammograms in the oxygen-saturated EHC@tartaric solution, it can be observed that the progression of jp and jORR diverges from the trend observed in EHC@citric and EHC@malic. It suggests that additional reactions, beyond reactions (1) and (2), take place:
a)
To explain the decrease in jORR without a corresponding reduction in jp (Fig. 5c-ii), it is proposed that [EHCox-O2]•− may continue to interact (via adsorption or chemical reaction) with O₂, acting as an oxygen scavenger. This is followed by an increase in jRRO without a corresponding increase in jp (Fig. 5c-iii, 5c-v), consistent with the subsequent transformation of the adduct into the superoxide radical anion, as described in reaction 2b.
b)
To account for the decrease in jp without a corresponding decrease in jORR (Fig. 5c-iv), it is proposed that [EHCred] •− also reacts with the H₂O₂ produced in reaction (2). This hypothesis is supported by the observed reactivity of EHC with hydrogen peroxide (Fig. 6S).

In summary, several competing reactions are proposed in an aqueous medium, with their relative rates likely dependent on the stability of [EHCred] •− and [EHCox-O2]•− species, which, in turn, are influenced by the nature of the electrolyte. The overall set of results suggests that the [EHCox-O2]•− adduct is better stabilized when formed in the citrate buffer solution.

At this stage, we are unable to fully rationalize this behaviour. The greater stabilization of the [EHCox-O2]•− species is likely associated with the type and number of intermolecular bonds between the OH moiety of the nanomaterial and the electrolyte. According to the literature, hydrogen bonding accounts for the varying reactivity of semiquinone isomers with O₂ [17], as well as the reactivity of the same semiquinone in different media [17–19]. However, the electrolyte itself influences the nanomaterial's structure during the synthesis process, which in turn impacts its ability to stabilize the unpaired electrons. Hence, the contribution of these two effects —electrolyte interactions and nanomaterials structure— cannot be easily separated regarding their effect on the radical stability. An upcoming publication will further investigate the relationship between the electrolyte's influence during synthesis and the resulting nanomaterial structures.

To evaluate the effect of electrolyte removal on the electrochemical behaviour of the different EHC nanomaterials, glassy carbon electrodes were modified with dialysed EHC@citric, EHC@malic, and EHC@tartaric solutions using the drop-casting method. The electrode modification process was performed under an argon flow and the voltammograms were recorded in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution at pH 7. For comparison purposes, the voltammograms of an unmodified electrode were also recorded in the same electrolytic solution. The results reveal an electrochemical response that is markedly different from that observed with the non-dialysed samples, but that resembles the dialysed EHC solution prepared in phosphate buffer (Fig. 7S). Notably, all the EHC nanomaterials are converted into an oxidised form, which is electrochemically characterised by a redox pair of peaks: a sharp reduction peak at ≅ − 0.34 V (peak Ic) and a broad oxidation peak at ≅ − 0.11 (peak Ia), Fig. 6 . Note that ΔEp is approximately 0.23 V, indicating a low electron transfer kinetics. Although the peaks are asymmetrical, they exhibit nearly the same charge (Table 1S), excepting in the EHC@tartaric . Upon cycling, the charge gradually decreases, until it disappears. This behaviour indicates that the electroactive fraction of the dialysed nanomaterial on the surface electrode gradually dissolves into the aqueous solution. The high-water solubility of these nanomaterials prevented the performance of diagnostic tests to fully characterize the reaction mechanism, particularly the number of electrons and protons involved in the electrode reaction.

Upon electrolyte removal, the conversion of the nanomaterial from a reduced to an oxidised form shows the crucial role of the electrolyte in stabilising semiquinone species, preventing its oxidation and/or participation in other inter- and intramolecular reactions. Oxidation may be driven by oxygen dissolved in the solution or from atmospheric exposure, despite efforts to minimize the solution´s contact with O2. The protective “shielding” effect of the electrolyte is likely due to strong intermolecular forces, such as hydrogen bonds, established between the functional groups of the carbon nanomaterial and those of the electrolyte.

The chemical composition of the EHC nanomaterials after dialysis was characterised by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The C 1s spectrum of EHC@malic is shown in Fig. 7 a, being representative of the resulting simultaneous fitting performed in all samples (All C 1s fitted regions are included in Fig. 8S). Fig. 7 also includes a table with the atomic concentrations of the main elements in the three samples, computed from the peak areas of O 1s, C 1s, and Na 1s, along with the atomic ratios. The sum of trace relative amounts of other elements, such as N, Cl, and Ca are also presented. Relevant XPS regions and wide spectra of all samples are shown in Figs. 9S and 10S.

To assess whether the O 1s peak includes contributions from sources other than oxygen bonded to carbon, the O(Cox)/Ototal ratio was estimated, where O(Cox) represents oxygen bonded to carbon. For this calculation a C:O stoichiometry of 1:1 is assumed at 288 eV (associated with C=O groups), and 1:2 at 289 eV (consistent with carboxylic or/and ester groups). For the 286.6 eV peak, the calculation was performed under two limiting scenarios: assuming a C:O stoichiometry of 2:1 which reflects the presence of C-O-C or/and epoxide groups, or a 1:1 stoichiometry, which corresponds to C-OH groups.

Notably, it can be concluded that the majority of oxygen in EHC@citric is not covalently bonded to carbon. In contrast, EHC@tartaric shows the highest amount of oxygen bonded to carbon (minimum O(Cox)/Ototal - 0.79). This result strongly supports the hypothesis that the superoxide adduct is more stabilised in EHC@citric, and less stabilised in EHC@tartaric. The ratio (O-O(Cox))/Na in EHC@citric aligns with the hypothesis that oxygen-containing groups not bonded to carbon are negatively charged, consistent with the formation of superoxide adduct balanced by sodium cations. Moreover, it demonstrates that, despite the electrolyte´s role in stabilising semiquinone, the superoxide adduct in EHC@citric remains stable even in an electrolyte-free EHC solution.

To effectively assess the impact of the electrolyte on the oxidized state of carbon, the O(Cox)/C ratio was also determined. This parameter provides a better insight than Ototal/C, which shows minimal variation with changes in the electrolyte where the nanomaterial was generated. The O(Cox)/C ratio suggests that, after the electrolyte removal, carbon is in a more reduced state in EHC@citric, while in EHC@tartaric and EHC@malic, it becomes more oxidized. Regardless of these differences, all three samples display the same electroactive species form.

Monitoring the electroactive form of EHC over time was the key to understanding the redox dynamics of reactions involving the electron-donating carbon-based nanomaterial electrogenerated from graphite. It was concluded that, at the end of the electrogeneration process in citric, tartaric, or malic buffers, three distinct quinoid-type forms coexist: the semiquinone-like (electroactive), the quinone-superoxide ion adduct (non-electroactive), and an oxidised form (non-electroactive). Their relative proportions vary depending on their stability, which is strongly influenced by the nature of the electrolyte.

The highest fraction of electroactive nanomaterial is generated in tartaric buffer, while the lowest is produced in malic buffer. In tartaric buffer, the predominant form is semiquinone; in the citric buffer, it is the superoxide-quinone adduct, and in the malic buffer, the nanomaterial is predominantly in an oxidised form. The results also indicate that the two entities, semiquinone, and superoxide-quinone adduct, can convert into each other in a redox cycle mediated by molecular oxygen. Due to the electron-donating properties and radical character of the EHC, it is envisaged that these nanomaterials must be prone to antioxidant applications.

A.D. Veloso: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. A.M. Ferraria: Investigation, Formal analysis. R.A. Videira: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. M.C. Oliveira: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Andreia D. Veloso thanks FCT, MCTES, ESF, and EU through the individual research Ph.D. for the Ph.D. scholarship (SFRH/BD/138425/2018). Romeu A. Videira thanks FCT for funding through CEECINST/00136/2021/CP2820/CT0001 DOI 10.54499/CEECINST/00136/2021/CP2820/CT0001

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2025.146071.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advanced carbon electrode materials for molecular electrochemistry
